evidence: goseg/config/config.go uses a per-field read-modify-write pattern (`setRuntimeContextBasePath`, `setRuntimeContextDebugMode`, etc.) over an atomic pointer, so independent mutators can overwrite each other instead of composing a single coherent runtime snapshot

goseg/main.go and goseg/startuporchestrator/startup_orchestrator.go read process context (`config.RuntimeContextSnapshot`) and directly consume package-level state instead of requiring an explicit context abstraction in constructor inputs

goseg/shipworkflow/recovery.go accepts a raw function-pointer runtime (`wireguardRecoveryRuntime`) and invokes callbacks directly without any validation, unlike other runtimes in this batch that validate callback groups through seam checks

Across ship transition handling, similar config-mutation + cleanup + restart sequences are duplicated in multiple handlers and helpers instead of being composed through a single transition policy abstraction.

`runTogglePowerTransition` and `runToggleNetworkTransition` both inline state checks, persist config updates, cleanup calls, and conditional container starts rather than delegating to a shared orchestration helper.
Different transition entry points (`buildToggleDevModeSteps`, `buildRuntimeConfigIntSteps`, `buildTogglePowerSteps`, `runToggle...Transition`) repeatedly encode the same lifecycle contract manually.
`parseStartupOptions` in `goseg/main.go` logs malformed CLI values (e.g., `http-port`, `ws-port`) and continues with default configuration instead of surfacing a startup failure.

`failUploadRequest` in `goseg/importer/importer.go` returns potentially meaningful errors from publish/response paths, but call sites in the same file can suppress those errors based on `responseWritten`, creating inconsistent observability and recovery behavior.

`WriteWgConf` in `goseg/docker/orchestration/wireguard.go` short-circuits on some read failures while `artifactwriter.Write` has alternate recovery paths in `goseg/docker/orchestration/internal/artifactwriter/artifact_writer.go`, resulting in non-uniform error treatment across failure modes.

findings.summary: Config runtime context setters are separate read-modify-write operations on a shared atomic pointer, which can drop updates under concurrent or interleaved calls.
evidence: `setRuntimeContextBasePath`, `setRuntimeContextDebugMode`, and peers all load `RuntimeContextSnapshot()`, mutate one field, then store the updated struct.
Because each setter writes the full struct independently, concurrent callers can overwrite unrelated fields (for example, basePath and architecture) with stale snapshots.

Multiple tests and call sites mutate runtime context via these setters (for example `SetBasePath`, `SetDebugMode`) indicating this path is part of runtime dependency control, not a single-threaded one-off initialization.
findings.suggestion: Refactor runtime-context updates to serialize through a single `updateRuntimeContext` helper (mutex-protected or CAS loop) that applies a full mutate function to the current snapshot, then stores once; keep public setters as thin wrappers over that shared updater.

findings.summary: Wireguard recovery uses a function-field seam without validation, creating an abstraction that can be instantiated in an invalid state and panic at runtime.
evidence: `RecoverWireguardFleet` accepts `wireguardRecoveryRuntime` directly and immediately calls `runtime.GetShipStatusFn`, `RestartContainerFn`, `WaitForShipExitFn`, etc. without nil checks.
The function type is exported via `NewWireguardRecoveryRuntime`, but callers can bypass it with a zero-value or partially filled struct because the public entry point does not enforce required seams.
Other runtime compositions in this batch (for example workflow/runtime constructors) validate callback groups before use, while this seam has no comparable guard.
findings.suggestion: Introduce a runtime validator for wireguard recovery (required callback set) and change `RecoverWireguardFleet` to obtain runtime via a constructor that returns an error on missing callbacks, or perform explicit nil checks at entry.

findings.summary: Ship transition handlers duplicate config mutation, cleanup, and restart orchestration instead of sharing a common policy helper.
evidence: `buildRuntimeConfigIntSteps` and `buildToggleDevModeSteps` both persist a section via `persistShipUrbitSectionConfig`, call `urbitCleanDelete`, then conditionally invoke `docker.StartContainer`.

`runTogglePowerTransition` and `runToggleNetworkTransition` also embed persistence/cleanup/start logic directly with branching, mixing transition decision logic and execution policy in the same function.

Handlers build transition runners (`buildTogglePowerSteps`, `buildToggleNetworkSteps`, and related entrypoints) that each re-encode similar orchestration flow instead of delegating to a reusable transition executor.
findings.suggestion: Extract a reusable transition execution helper (for example, `runShipConfigTransition`) that takes a typed strategy: (a) optional status resolution, (b) section-specific mutation, (c) post-mutate cleanup/restart policy. Then have both handlers and transition builders compose that helper so transition semantics are explicit and centrally maintained.

findings.summary: Startup accepts invalid CLI port values silently by falling back to defaults.
evidence: `parseStartupOptions` parses `--http-port` and `--ws-port` with `strconv.Atoi` and on conversion failure only logs an error while continuing; no error is returned to the main startup flow.
The same function populates `listenAddr`/`wsAddr` from defaults after failed parsing, so invalid input can start a server on unintended ports without explicit operator feedback.
findings.suggestion: Return an error from `parseStartupOptions` for invalid user-supplied port flags and abort startup with a clear message; alternatively collect all parse errors and fail fast before listener startup, and keep the default path only when flags are absent.

findings.summary: Upload error handling can drop backend failures when a response has already been written.
evidence: `failUploadRequest` returns errors via named propagation from response publishing/write operations.

Callers guard handling with `if err != nil && !responseWritten`, so when `responseWritten` is true, upstream transport/publish errors are not surfaced through the same error path and are effectively downgraded to silent failure.
findings.suggestion: Split the operation into explicit stages: write response status once, then independently handle/return backend-side failures via explicit logging + metrics + caller-visible error return; ensure both paths are tested (response-written and non-response-written) in `importer_test.go`.

findings.summary: Wireguard artifact write path applies different recovery strategies depending on filesystem read error class.
evidence: `WriteWgConf` first attempts `os.ReadFile` and only on `os.ErrNotExist` performs fallback behavior.

For other read failures (e.g., permission or transient I/O), the function returns immediately without attempting the fallback write path that exists in artifact writer logic, resulting in inconsistent outcomes for equivalent failure intent.
findings.suggestion: Unify recovery semantics: on any read failure, attempt a bounded fallback path via `artifactwriter.Write` and include context-rich error wrapping so callers can distinguish source-read issues from generation/write failures.
